---
title: "Three Skim-Traps, the GMU Theme, and PowerPoint"
date: "2026-08-10"
---

# Three Skim-Traps, the GMU Theme, and PowerPoint {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Week Eight, Lab Eight, part three of ten. In part two we built the poster's six-panel skeleton and the rule that it reads numbers but never contains them. Now we dwell on the three lines in that skeleton you will get wrong if you skim — and the two alternate paths, the real GMU theme and PowerPoint. These are small details, but each one has bitten a real build, including the verification of this very lab.
:::

## Three Lines You Will Get Wrong

- `\input{abstract.tex}` and `\includegraphics{...}` — generated, cannot drift
- The **QR code is not decoration** — it links the floor to the packet
- A broken QR shows a 404 on a reviewer's phone — irreparable damage
- `beamerposter` size is in **centimeters**: 48"×36" is `121.92, 91.44`
- Wrong units → microscopic poster or "dimension too large"

::: notes
Three things in this skeleton are worth dwelling on, because they are exactly the lines you get wrong if you skim. First, the two load-bearing kinds of line — input-of-abstract and include-graphics for every figure — generated by code so they cannot drift from the headline. Second, the QR code is not decoration; it is the load-bearing connection from the symposium floor to the replication packet. Generate it from your actual public repo URL, and verify it scans with your phone before the symposium. A broken QR resolves to "this page does not exist" on a visiting reviewer's phone, and that credibility damage is irreparable. Third, a subtle compile trap that bit the verification of this lab: beamerposter's custom width and height are in centimeters, not inches. Forty-eight by thirty-six inches landscape is width one-twenty-one-point-nine-two, height ninety-one-point-four-four — not forty-eight, thirty-six. Get the units wrong and you get either a microscopic poster or a build that fails with "dimension too large."
:::

## The GMU Theme and the PowerPoint Path

- Real GMU theme: change only `\usetheme{}` and maybe page size
- The six-panel body is identical — learn the shape on the default theme
- PowerPoint: insert figures via **Insert → Picture → Linked**, never pasted
- Abstract pasted from the **generated** `abstract.txt`, never typed
- Deliverable is `paper/poster.pdf` — must regenerate from `make poster`

::: notes
Two notes on flexibility. When the College of Science distributes its current academic-poster template — and you should confirm the URL with the cosassip email; that is a CHECK tag in the lab — the only changes are the use-theme line and possibly the page-size constants. The body is identical: the make-title, the abstract block, the six-panel column structure, the input and include-graphics lines. So learn the shape on the default theme now and swap to the GMU theme when the template lands. And if you prefer to design the poster in PowerPoint, because the visual feedback is faster, the rule is the same: every figure on the slide is the same file your notebooks generated, inserted via Insert, Picture, Linked — not pasted — so a regenerated figure refreshes when you re-export to PDF. The abstract block is pasted from the generated plain-text mirror, never typed. The deliverable is paper-slash-poster-dot-pdf, and how it was authored matters less than that it regenerates from make-poster with no manual edits.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Three skim-traps: the generated `\input` lines, a QR that must actually scan, and **centimeter** poster units. Swapping in the GMU theme touches only `\usetheme`; PowerPoint works too if figures are *linked*, not pasted. Next: compiling the PDF.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part three: remember the three skim-traps — the generated input and include-graphics lines, the QR code that must actually scan to the public repo, and the centimeter units on the poster size. The real GMU theme changes only the use-theme line, so learn the shape on the default. And PowerPoint is a fine authoring tool as long as figures are linked, not pasted, and the abstract comes from the generated mirror. In part four we actually compile this thing — Overleaf in the browser and latexmk or tectonic locally.
:::
